note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-09-02-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): the Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 197.05s but the ffprobed master is 196.598s (0.45s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 14 blocks concatenated with an exact 0.400s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations are inflated ~0.03s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates to -0.45s by s14, which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (every onset landed on the 0.08s mp3-frame grid); final end = ffprobed master duration 196.598s.

phrases.text: Open Digital, and stay on Mobile App Users.
The sidebar splits the customer's world in two: the portal, and the mobile app.
Under Mobile you get five pages.
This lesson visits all of them, but you only read.
Nothing here gets saved.
Four numbers run across the top.
Total users, active this month, and the split between iOS and Android.
That last pair earns its keep on a support call.
It tells you, before you ask, which phone your customer is probably holding.
Three cards fill in the shape.
One tracks growth over the year.
One breaks the base down by device.
The last sorts users into active, monthly, and gone quiet.
A rising inactive slice is worth a second look.
Now the list itself.
Seven columns per user: name, email, app version, platform, last active, notifications, and status.
Two of those seven do most of the troubleshooting work.
Read one row like a support ticket.
App version tells you whether they're stuck behind a fix.
Notifications, on or off, tells you why a customer swears the alert never came.
Start here, before you touch anything else.
Above the table, the usual controls.
Search pulls up one customer by name or email.
There's also Export and a New Record button.
Leave both alone today; reading the list is all a technician needs.
Now the pages that build the app.
Open Mobile App Branding.
Notice the shell flips to Settings.
This is where a brand's name, logo, colors, and support details get set,
with a live phone preview alongside.
Four blocks, top to bottom.
Tenant, brand, colors, support.
Display names and taglines, logo links,
the primary and accent colors, a support phone and email.
Editing any of it and publishing would re-skin the real app, so we only look.
Back to the group, open Mobile App Features.
These are on-and-off switches for the fun: daily spin, wash streak, scratch cards, a few more,
plus wallet and gift cards.
Flip one and the preview updates.
We flip nothing.
Last page, Push Notifications.
It opens as Push Campaigns: write a message, pick an audience,
watch it land on a lock-screen preview.
That's marketing's job, not ours.
We create no campaign here, and we send nothing.
One thing ties these pages together.
Each shows a live preview beside the settings,
so you can see what the customer sees without leaving the console.
A standalone preview launcher isn't in this build, so we've parked it.
Click Close Settings.
The menu folds away and drops you back on the platform,
with nothing saved, nothing published, and nothing sent.
One entry stays closed: Mobile Feedback.
It's listed in the group, but the page didn't load in our capture.
Until someone walks it live, treat what's inside as unconfirmed.
That's the mobile corner of Digital.
One page you open on real calls, Mobile App Users,
and three you'll recognize but leave alone.
When a customer's app misbehaves, you already know the first two columns to read.
